one_line_takeaway: A broad, up-to-date but purely narrative survey of zero-shot deepfake detection and source-side prevention -- useful as a taxonomy and reading map, but it contributes no experiments of its own and its headline accuracy numbers are all borrowed from the works it reviews.
key_finding: The survey's synthesis argument: self-supervised and zero-shot approaches remove the dependence on large labeled deepfake datasets and are the more promising route to generalizing against unseen/evolving generators, but detection alone is insufficient -- the authors argue for an integrated, multi-layered defense that pairs zero-shot detection with proactive prevention (adversarial perturbation of source media, watermarking, blockchain provenance, real-time monitoring). No quantitative claim of the authors' own supports this; it is an argued position drawn from the surveyed literature. A recurring cited weakness across surveyed detectors is overfitting to manipulation-specific artifacts and sharp accuracy drops on unseen manipulation types.
core_contribution: This is a survey / literature-review manuscript (submitted to a Foundations and Trends in Signal Processing template) on zero-shot visual deepfake detection AND proactive deepfake prevention. Its own contribution is organizational, not experimental: the authors (Sar, Roy, Choudhury, Abraham; UPES/Sai University) map the deepfake-generation landscape (GANs, transformers, diffusion, real-time apps), then build a taxonomy of zero-shot DETECTION families -- contrastive self-supervised learning (SimCLR, MoCo, BYOL), self-supervised anomaly detection (autoencoders, one-class SVM, out-of-distribution detection, temporal-inconsistency analysis), and generative-model fingerprinting for attribution (pixel-level, frequency-domain, latent-space) -- and a parallel taxonomy of PREVENTION strategies (adversarial perturbations to disrupt generators, digital watermarking, blockchain content verification, real-time AI monitoring). It closes with a challenges section and future directions (explainable AI, multimodal fusion, quantum AI, federated learning). The paper presents NO original model, dataset, or experiment of its own; all numeric results it cites belong to prior works it surveys.
threat_model: Surveyed (not novel to this paper): adversaries use GAN- and diffusion-based generators plus real-time face-swap / voice-clone tools to produce synthetic media that (1) bypasses biometric authentication (facial/voice recognition in banking), (2) enables financial fraud and phishing by impersonating executives/officials, (3) spreads political misinformation and propaganda, and (4) produces non-consensual explicit content. The paper stresses that supervised detectors fail to generalize to unseen generators, motivating zero-shot detection that needs no labeled fake samples, and 'prevention-at-source' that disrupts generation before content exists. The paper does not define a formal or novel threat model with an attacker capability boundary; it uses the threat landscape as narrative motivation for the survey.
limitations: (1) No original contribution beyond the review structure -- no new method, dataset, benchmark, or experiment. (2) Table XVII's numbers are third-party results under heterogeneous, non-comparable conditions (mixed ACC vs AUC, different datasets, some rows blank) and are reproduced without a unified re-evaluation, so they cannot be read as evidence for any single approach. (3) The paper repeatedly identifies the ABSENCE of standardized zero-shot benchmarks as an open problem yet does not itself propose or run one. (4) Low editorial polish: the published abstract is fragmentary/garbled (it begins mid-sentence and ends with dangling Scopus counts), there are typos and a broken citation token ('[?]'), which weakens confidence in transcription of the surveyed figures. (5) Prevention claims (watermarking, blockchain, adversarial perturbation) are described conceptually with known robustness caveats (perturbations transfer poorly to unseen generators) but not empirically validated.

datasets_used: No datasets were used by the authors themselves (survey paper).

Datasets referenced within the surveyed works (Table XVII): FaceForensics++ (FF++), Celeb-DF / Celeb-DF(v2), DFDC / DFDCP, CelebA-HQ, CelebA, UADFV, VoxCeleb, FFHQ, LSUN, OpenForensics, and generator-source sets ProGAN / SNGAN / MMDGAN / CramerGAN / StyleGAN / StyleGAN2 / BigGAN / CycleGAN / StarGAN.
quantitative_results: SURVEYED (cited, not authors' own) Table XVII: Encoder-Decoder [ref 310, 2021] on FF++ + DFDC = 99.90% ACC.
SURVEYED Table XVII: Recycle-GAN [ref 315, 2021] on Viper = 99.39% ACC.
SURVEYED Table XVII: One-class Autoencoder [ref 309, 2020] on FF++ = 97.50% ACC.
SURVEYED Table XVII: Encoder-Decoder [ref 76, 2021] on UADFV + FF++ + Celeb-DF = 97.60% AUC.
SURVEYED Table XVII: GAN detector [ref 320, 2024] on FF++ + Celeb-DF = 98.22% ACC (noted to underperform baseline ADD on DF/FS).
SURVEYED Table XVII: GAN detector [ref 319, 2024] on CelebA/ProGAN/SNGAN/MMDGAN/CramerGAN = 97.02% ACC.
SURVEYED Table XVII: forensic GAN-artifact + CNN [ref 312, 2019] = 72.64% ACC (CelebA) / 83.64% ACC (LSUN); GAN-artifact + SVM [ref 313, 2019] = 0.70 AUC (GAN Crop) / 0.61 AUC (GAN Full).
SURVEYED Table XVII: MRI-GAN + SSIM [ref 316, 2022] on DFDC/Celeb/FDF/FFHQ = 74% test ACC (91% on plain frames).
REPORTED market figure [market.us, ref 16]: global deepfake-AI market USD 550 Million (2023) projected to USD 18,989.4 Million by 2033, CAGR 42.5%.
REPORTED societal stat: over 96% of deepfakes posted online are non-consensual explicit content, disproportionately targeting women.
REPORTED bibliometric (Scopus, Fig.3, cited in abstract): in 2024, 632 studies on deepfake creation, 2,910 on detection, 3,474 total.
REPORTED case study: 2019 UK-Germany CEO voice-clone scam, EUR 220,000 (~USD 243,000) fraudulently transferred.
REPORTED physiological baseline used for blink-anomaly detection: natural human blink rate 15-20 blinks/minute.
ILLUSTRATIVE artifact: Robert Downey Jr. -> Rowan Atkinson face-swap clip split into 657 frames at 26 fps, manually inspected for inconsistencies (Fig.11).
baselines_compared: No baselines run by the authors. The survey qualitatively contrasts self-supervised/contrastive methods (SimCLR, MoCo, BYOL) against supervised learning in a capability matrix (Table VI), and self-supervised anomaly-detection methods (autoencoder, one-class SVM, OOD, temporal) against supervised learning (Table XI). Comparisons are binary yes/no properties, not measured performance.
